sanskrit_devanagari:
भूमिरापोऽनलो वायुः खं मनो बुद्धिरेव च ।
अहङ्कार इतीयं मे भिन्ना प्रकृतिरष्टधा ॥ ४ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
bhūmir āpo 'nalo vāyuḥ khaṁ mano buddhir eva ca |
ahaṅkāra itīyaṁ me bhinnā prakṛtir aṣṭadhā || 4 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical).
exact (academic edition; text body identical; punctuation/danda rendering differs).
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_07.004 = MBh_06,029.004). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: Earth, water, fire, air, ether, mind, intelligence and false ego – all together these eight constitute My separated material energies.
Earth, water, fire, air, space, mind, intellect, and ego—these are eight components of My material energy.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The science of God analyzes the constitutional position of God and His diverse energies. Material nature is called prakṛti, or the energy of the Lord in His different puruṣa incarnations (expansions).

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.4 Iyam, this; prakṛtiḥ, Prakṛti, [Prakṛti here does not mean the Pradhāna of the Sāṅkhyas.] the divine power called Māyā; me, of Mine, as described; bhinnā, is divided; aṣṭadhā, eight-fold; iti, thus: bhūmiḥ, earth — not the gross earth but the subtle element called earth.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara explicitly distinguishes this prakṛti from Sāṅkhya's Pradhāna. The eight elements named are subtle elements (tanmātras), not gross — and the whole eightfold is Krishna's māyā-śakti, his divine power, not an independent ontological substrate.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.4 Know that Prakṛti, the material cause of this universe, which consists of endless varieties of objects and means of enjoyment and places of enjoyment, is divided into eightfold substances — earth, water, fire, air and ether, having smell, taste etc., as their attributes.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads prakṛti as the actual material cause — the substance from which objects, enjoyers, and locations of enjoyment are constituted. The eightfold list is the standard Vaiśeṣika/Sāṅkhya ontology of subtle constituents that compose the lower order of being.

positions.rendering: eight separated material energies (with 'false ego' for ahaṅkāra)
the eight elements are subtle, not gross; the whole is Krishna's māyā-śakti, not Sāṅkhya's Pradhāna
actual material cause; eightfold substances with smell/taste etc. as attributes

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All four agree on the eightfold list. They disagree on what kind of cause this prakṛti is. Shankara explicitly disowns the Sāṅkhya Pradhāna reading and reads prakṛti as Krishna's māyā-śakti (his divine power) — the elements named are subtle (tanmātras). Ramanuja reads prakṛti as actual material cause with substantive constituents. Prabhupada's 'false ego' for ahaṅkāra is a translation choice that is doctrinally loaded (the ego is not 'false' for Ramanuja; it is real but lower-order). Mukundananda's neutral 'ego' is more cautious.

literal_meaning: Earth, water, fire, air, space, mind, intellect, and ego — thus this prakṛti of mine is divided eightfold.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names his lower (aparā) prakṛti, the eightfold material substrate of phenomenal existence: the five gross/subtle elements (earth, water, fire, air, space), and the three psychic faculties (mind, intellect, ego). The list is canonical and recurs across Sanskrit philosophical literature; the chapter introduces it here to set up the contrast with the higher (parā) prakṛti named in 7.5. The verse is structural — it names the categories — not metaphysical-revelatory yet; that comes in 7.5-7.7.
